reasoning: Mexico arrive on an eight-game unbeaten run in 2026, having beaten Serbia 5-1 and conceded only twice across the entire year, with a settled defensive partnership and dangerous set-piece threat via Montes and Vásquez. The Estadio Azteca altitude (~2,240m) is a genuine physical tax on South Africa, who are a sea-level team returning to the World Cup after a 16-year absence and whose pre-tournament warmups produced only a draw with Nicaragua and a draw with Jamaica.

South Korea hold a modest edge rooted in superior warm-up form (6-0 over two friendlies vs Czech's 5-2), stronger top-end squad quality around Son Heung-min and Kim Min-jae, and a meaningful altitude advantage at Guadalajara (~1,566m) where Czech Republic, based in sea-level Dallas, face acclimatisation costs. Czech defensive fragility was exposed even against non-qualifier Guatemala, and their 20-year World Cup absence adds pressure, though Schick and Soucek make them a genuine set-piece threat. The match remains close — a draw is nearly as likely as a Korea win — and the 1-0 scoreline reflects a tight, low-scoring opener where a single moment of Son or Lee Kang-in quality tips the balance.

Canada enter as the stronger side on paper (FIFA rank ~31 vs. Bosnia's 65) with home-crowd backing at a sold-out BMO Field where they have lost just once in 28 matches, and key attackers Jonathan David and Cyle Larin fully fit; however, captain Alphonso Davies is officially ruled out and Moïse Bombito is also absent, stripping the back-line and left flank of real quality.

USA hold a meaningful edge at home at SoFi with the crowd, a superior FIFA ranking, and a recent 2-1 friendly win over this same Paraguay side. The likely absence of Julio Enciso — Paraguay's most dangerous attacker — blunts their already limited creativity (fewest CONMEBOL qualifying goals), while Chris Richards returning to anchor the US backline is a further boost. However, Paraguay's ultra-defensive 4-2-3-1 under Alfaro makes them hard to break down, World Cup group openers frequently end level, and USA's pre-tournament defensive leakiness (conceding 4 in the two friendlies without Richards) warrants caution on backing a comfortable home win.

Switzerland hold a commanding edge across every factor: a 37-place FIFA ranking gap, a settled top-club core (Akanji, Xhaka, Freuler, Embolo, Kobel) with no injury concerns, and an unbeaten qualifying run conceding just two goals. Qatar arrive in dire form — winless in their last five, scoreless over 373 minutes, with key March friendlies cancelled and a squad almost entirely drawn from the Qatari domestic league. The venue is neutral and at sea level, removing any thermal/altitude equaliser.

Brazil hold a genuine talent edge with Vinicius Jr., Raphinha and Alisson, but the losses of Rodrygo, Estêvão and Militão meaningfully thin their attacking and defensive depth, and Neymar's fitness at 34 is uncertain. Morocco arrive as AFCON champions, unbeaten in 29 matches, with an elite defensive structure and Hakimi fit after his Champions League triumph — though they too are weakened by the last-minute losses of Aguerd and Ezzalzouli.

Scotland arrive in dominant form — back-to-back 4-0 wins over Curacao and Bolivia — with Ché Adams, McTominay and Shankland all firing, and hold a 45-place FIFA ranking advantage over Haiti. However, Scotland are missing five first-choice players (Gilmour, Hickey, Doak, Souttar, Christie), which hollows out both their best XI and bench depth. Haiti showed they can be dangerous on the counter with pace, and their motivation to take three points from their most winnable group game is acute. The draw probability is nudged above the market line to reflect Scotland's injury-hit squad and Haiti's potential to nick something via transition play, but Scotland's class should ultimately prevail.

Türkiye hold a clear edge in squad quality — a rated attack of 79 vs Australia's 67, with Güler fit and Çalhanoğlu available — and arrive in better recent form (W-W-D-L-W vs Australia's W-D-L-W-D). Australia's injury list is meaningful: the absences of first-choice midfielder McGree and wing-back Miller disrupt Popovic's preferred XI, while Souttar returns from 18 months out and is match-rusty. Vancouver's sea-level, neutral venue cancels any climate edge, and both sides face equivalent travel.

Germany enter on a nine-match winning run with an elite attacking trident of Wirtz, Musiala and Havertz fully fit, and Neuer confirmed available after a calf scare. Curaçao (FIFA 82nd) are World Cup debutants whose preparation included a 4-1 loss to Scotland; their plan will be a deep low block, but the quality and depth gap is enormous. Houston is sea-level and neutral to both sides, offering Germany no venue penalty.

Netherlands hold clear advantages in squad depth and FIFA ranking (7th vs 19th), but arrive with significant absences — Xavi Simons (ACL), Jurrien Timber (groin, tournament out), and de Ligt — blunting their creativity and defensive options. Japan enter in exceptional form with six consecutive friendly wins, including a historic 1-0 win over England, and retain Endo, Kubo, and Kamada despite losing Mitoma and Minamino. The AT&T Stadium's indoor, sea-level setting neutralises any environmental edge.

Ecuador arrive in excellent shape — a 19-match unbeaten run, the meanest defence in CONMEBOL qualifying (5 goals conceded in 18 games), and elite spine in Caicedo, Pacho, and Hincapié all fit — making them rightful favourites despite being the nominal away side. Ivory Coast are dangerous on the counter with Amad Diallo and Simon Adingra, and their morale is sky-high after beating France 2-1 in their final tune-up, but they lack a clinical No.9 (Haller omitted) and have historically struggled in the World Cup group stage.

Sweden hold a clear squad quality edge, with Gyökeres and Isak forming one of the tournament's most dangerous strike partnerships, and their play-off wins over Ukraine and Poland showed they can score freely in competitive pressure matches. Tunisia's warm-up form is genuinely alarming — a 5-0 loss to Belgium (admittedly with 10 men for 30 minutes) and a 1-0 defeat to Austria — raising real questions about their defensive readiness against European pace and pressing. However, Tunisia's flawless CAF qualifying record (10 clean sheets in 10) shows their defensive structure is real in competitive contexts, and MD1 opener caution means a tighter match is plausible.

Spain are reigning European champions with a world-class squad built around Yamal, Pedri, Rodri and Nico Williams, entered the tournament unbeaten in their last 10+ matches and are heavy favourites in every metric; their only notable injury absentee is Fermín López (fractured metatarsal), while Yamal and Nico Williams were rested as a precaution in the final warm-up but are expected fit for MD1. Cape Verde are first-time World Cup participants ranked 69th, lack individual star power or a world-class striker, and face a massive quality gap; their pre-tournament form (3-0 vs Bermuda, 3-0 vs Serbia) is encouraging but the level of opposition is incomparable.

Belgium enter on a 13-game unbeaten run, including a 5-0 warm-up rout of Tunisia, and boast clear squad superiority through De Bruyne, Doku, and Tielemans. However, Lukaku's severe lack of match fitness and De Bruyne's recent return from surgery introduce real attacking bluntness, while Courtois remains a doubt. Egypt arrive with an elite counter-attacking pairing in a less-than-fully-fit Salah and a sharp Marmoush, backed by a defence that conceded only twice in 10 qualifying matches — a setup tailor-made to frustrate and punish on the break.

Uruguay hold a clear squad-quality edge with Valverde, Ugarte and a La Liga-calibre defence, and the market's ~63% away probability is broadly justified. However, two significant factors compress the gap: Saudi Arabia arrive with genuine upset pedigree (Argentina 2022) and a new coach Donis who favours defensive solidity, while Uruguay enter the tournament with a chronic goalscoring crisis — just 2 goals in their last 360 minutes — and Ronald Araújo almost certainly absent through injury for this opener. Both sides have won just 1 of their last 5 matches, and Bielsa's high-press 4-3-3 versus a disciplined Saudi low-block is a recipe for a tight, low-scoring game.

Iran (FIFA ~20th) carry a significant quality edge over New Zealand (85th), with Taremi leading a settled, experienced attack and a defensively solid qualifying record of 11 wins in 16 games. New Zealand will sit deep — as they did in 2010 when they drew all three group games — but a 4-0 pre-tournament friendly loss to Haiti raises real questions about their ceiling against better opposition, and defender Pijnaker is a fitness concern. Iran's off-pitch disruptions (Tijuana base camp, visa saga, Azmoun's political exclusion) introduce marginal uncertainty, nudging the win probability only slightly above the book's 49.4%.

France possess a generational talent advantage — Mbappe, Dembele, Olise and Doue give Deschamps one of the most dangerous attacks in the tournament, and their pre-tournament wins over Brazil and Colombia confirm elite-level readiness. Senegal, however, are no pushover: Mane leads a disciplined, physically imposing side that has historically troubled France (2-0 all-time H2H), and their counter-attacking pace via Sarr and Mane can punish a French defence that conceded in both warm-up games.

Norway arrive with a fully fit squad including Haaland and a recently recovered Ødegaard, a perfect 8-win qualifying campaign, and superior quality at every position. Iraq's entire 26-man squad is available and their defensive shape under Arnold is disciplined, but the talent gap is enormous — Norway are 26 FIFA ranking places higher with a frontline of Haaland, Sørloth, and Nusa that Iraq have no answer for. The market's ~76% Norway win probability aligns tightly with the form, availability, and style-matchup evidence; a slight upward nudge to 77% is warranted given Ødegaard's confirmed fitness.

Argentina are heavy favourites as defending world champions with a deep squad featuring Lautaro Martínez, Mac Allister, Romero and Di María-era depth, though Messi is managing a left hamstring strain and did not feature in the 2-0 win over Honduras, while Balerdi has been ruled out entirely — if Messi is fit by June 17 this line-up is elite, if not it still outclasses Algeria by a wide margin.

Austria hold a decisive squad quality and tactical edge — a FIFA top-25 side with top Bundesliga talent, led by Rangnick's high-pressing 4-2-3-1 — against Jordan's World Cup debutants whose spine is largely domestic-league based. The loss of Baumgartner (thigh, warm-up injury) trims Austria's midfield quality, while Jordan are also missing their top scorer Alnemat (ACL), partially offset by Olwan's recovery. Jordan's compact 5-2-3 defensive block and Al-Tamari-led counters give them a credible upset path, nudging the draw probability slightly above the book line, but Austria's superior depth, organization, and high stakes motivation make a comfortable win the most likely outcome.

Portugal enter as reigning Nations League champions ranked 5th globally, with an elite midfield quartet of Fernandes, Bernardo Silva, Vitinha and João Neves and unbeaten in 12 of their last 13 matches including a 2-0 win over the USA and 2-1 over Chile; no significant injury absentees confirmed. DR Congo return to the World Cup for the first time in 52 years with a diaspora-built squad ranked 46th — Wissa's pace and Mbemba's defensive leadership are real threats but the squad quality gap is vast.

Ghana hold a clear squad-quality edge on paper — Semenyo, Iñaki Williams, Partey and Ayew outclass Panama's roster in individual talent — but their availability crisis (Kudus, Salisu, Djiku all absent; Partey carrying a groin concern) and dire recent form (one draw, five losses since October 2025) substantially erode that advantage. Panama arrive better-prepared, having played Brazil, Bosnia and the Dominican Republic in warm-ups, while Ghana's final tune-up vs Honduras was cancelled; Panama's low-block style at BMO Field (sea-level Toronto, no venue edge for either side) will make this tight and hard to break down.

Colombia carry a decisive squad quality edge — Luis Díaz (26 goals/23 assists at Bayern), James Rodríguez, and a settled Néstor Lorenzo system that went 28 games unbeaten and reached the 2024 Copa América final — against a World Cup debutant side whose pre-tournament form is patchy (lost 2-0 to Canada, trailed Netherlands 1-0 at 66' on June 8). The Azteca's 2,250m altitude is a genuine equaliser that rewards compact defending and counters, marginally inflating the draw probability versus sea level, but neither side is particularly altitude-adapted so the edge is mild.

England hold a clear squad-quality edge — ranked 4th globally with Kane, Saka, Bellingham, and Rice all available — but come in with an unsettled system, notable absentees (Foden, Palmer, TAA), and a modest pre-tournament record including friendly losses to Senegal and Japan. Croatia are formidable tournament opponents with a historically over-performing mentality, though key players Modrić (cheekbone, game-day doubt) and Gvardiol (returning from five months out) are less than fully sharp. The indoor AT&T Stadium neutralises heat/altitude factors for both sides.

Czechia carry a clear availability edge: they report zero injuries or suspensions while South Africa are missing two key midfielders (Sphephelo Sithole and Themba Zwane both suspended) and have a doubtful left-back in Modiba, which strips Bafana Bafana of experienced central presence and forces untested cover. Czechia's squad quality — Schick, Soucek, Coufal, Hlozek — is meaningfully higher at club level, and the stakes are mirror-image must-win for both sides after MD1 losses, so Czech pressure is no greater. The Atlanta venue is sea-level with no altitude handicap for either team, and both squads are on similar six-day rest cycles.

Switzerland are the clearly superior side on squad depth and tournament pedigree, but their MD1 performance (dominant yet conceding a 94th-minute own goal vs Qatar) reveals a tendency to sit back and invite pressure. Bosnia are a disciplined, low-block side with genuine set-piece threat who took the lead against Canada — they are no pushover. The book's 60.7% implied Swiss win probability looks slightly generous given Switzerland's unconvincing form (one win in their last four pre-tournament matches, a 4-3 loss to Germany) and Bosnia's organization; I trim Switzerland fractionally and redistribute toward draw and Bosnia.

Canada are the clear quality side at home in Vancouver with Jonathan David, Cyle Larin and a potentially returning Alphonso Davies all available, and the partisan BC Place crowd provides a significant intangible edge; the book's 73% home-win implied probability is broadly sound but nudged down slightly given Canada's recent clinical struggles — they dominated Bosnia yet needed a late Larin substitute appearance to salvage a draw, and Davies remains a fitness doubt.

Mexico carry a genuine altitude and home-crowd edge at Estadio Akron (1,566m), but the loss of CB César Montes to suspension forces a defensive reshuffle with Edson Álvarez dropping back and a teenager into midfield, moderately weakening their shape. South Korea arrive fully fit, with Son, Lee Kang-In and a proven counter-attacking threat that neutralises much of Mexico's home advantage. Crucially, a draw clinches near-certain qualification for both teams, creating strong tactical incentives to avoid defeat — pushing draw probability above the market's 0.295. The market's slight lean toward Mexico is preserved but narrowed, and the most likely single outcome is a 1-1 draw.

USA are the clear favorites at home in Seattle with a full squad available — Pulisic's calf concern appears manageable and no suspensions cloud the XI — after a dominant 4-1 opener that showcased depth and European-level attacking quality under Pochettino. Australia deserve genuine respect: their 2-0 shock over Turkey was backed by a disciplined 3-4-2-1 shape and eight saves from young keeper Beach, but Toure carries a calf doubt and the Socceroos face a significant step up in opponent quality with less margin for the low-block counter they exploited against Turkey.

Morocco (FIFA #6) carry a significant squad quality edge over Scotland, and their controlled 1-1 draw with Brazil showed genuine tournament-level capability; their 30-game unbeaten run is a powerful prior. Scotland's availability situation is worse than headlines suggest — Gannon-Doak, Gilmour, Souttar, and Christie are all out, blunting their creative depth — while Morocco's core (Hakimi, Saibari, Díaz, Bouaddi) appears largely intact despite the confirmed absences of Aguerd and Ezzalzouli. The venue (sea-level Boston, neutral crowd) removes any meaningful 'home' advantage for Scotland, and Morocco's superior individual quality across the pitch should translate into sustained pressure.

Brazil are overwhelming favorites here: a five-time world champion squad with elite European club talent against a Haiti side ranked 82nd who lost their opener to Scotland. The class gap is enormous, and Brazil will be highly motivated to respond to a stuttering draw against Morocco. The key caveats are Neymar's fitness doubt (calf injury, possible return), Casemiro's poor MD1 performance, and confirmed absences of Rodrygo, Estêvão and Militão, which leave Brazil a little light on the wings. Haiti's disciplined 4-4-2 block with Bellegarde and Isidor could frustrate briefly, but Brazil's depth — Vinicius Jr., Raphinha, Martinelli, Gabriel Jesus — should comfortably overwhelm them.

Turkey hold a clear squad-quality edge with elite European club talent (Güler, Çalhanoğlu, Yıldız), dominated Australia territorially with 30 shots but were wasteful — expect improved conversion against a Paraguay backline that conceded four to the USA and has five players on yellow-card tightropes. Paraguay's two injury doubts (Caballero, Sosa) further thin their options, and they historically lose half their World Cup games against UEFA nations.

Netherlands hold squad-depth and FIFA-ranking edges (ranked 7th, core of De Jong, Van Dijk, Gakpo, Reijnders intact) but their defensive fragility — five games without a clean sheet and two conceded to Japan — is a serious concern against the tournament's most dangerous strike pairing. Sweden's Isak and Gyökeres clicked immediately in their only competitive start together, and Potter's side arrive fully fit, high on confidence, and with genuine tactical discipline in a 3-5-2 that could suffocate the Dutch midfield.

Germany's squad quality, attacking firepower (Musiala, Havertz, Sane) and blistering 10-match win streak make them strong favourites, and Toronto at sea level offers no altitude penalty for either side. Ivory Coast are dangerous — Amad Diallo's late winner vs Ecuador and a pre-tournament scalp over France show genuine quality — but they carry a defensive concern in the doubtful Evan Ndicka and a thin, inexperienced strike corps without Haller. Germany's structured pressing shape exploits exactly the kind of transitional vulnerability Ivory Coast's attack-minded flanks leave behind.

Ecuador hold a massive squad quality advantage — with Caicedo, Pacho, Hincapie and Valencia all available and no fresh injury concerns — against a Curaçao side ranked 82nd in the world that was just routed 7-1 by Germany. Ecuador's MD1 loss to Ivory Coast was harsh (they were the better side, hit the woodwork, and had their 19-game unbeaten run ended by a stoppage-time goal), so La Tri will be highly motivated and desperate for three points. The main uncertainty is Ecuador's modest attacking efficiency under Beccacece; Curaçao's compact defensive block may frustrate briefly, but the class differential should tell comfortably.

Japan hold a clear structural edge in squad quality and FIFA ranking (18th vs 45th) and showed resilience drawing 2-2 with the Netherlands, but cumulative injuries to Endo, Mitoma, and now Kubo have meaningfully dented their attacking options. Tunisia's morale shock from a 5-1 hammering by Sweden is partially offset by the Renard appointment, which historically galvanises squads, and the Eagles have nothing to lose. However, a brand-new coaching staff with just days of preparation and a historically poor World Cup record make a Tunisian win very unlikely; Japan's superior organisation and depth should see them control the match and convert enough chances to win.

Spain are a class above Saudi Arabia on every structural metric — squad depth, FIFA ranking, top-club talent — and face a side whose only win in five came against Puerto Rico. The 0-0 opener against Cabo Verde is a genuine alarm bell for De la Fuente, creating a pressure cooker that should unlock a more aggressive, front-footed Spain with Yamal expected to feature more prominently; that urgency sharpens rather than derails them. Atlanta (sea level, summer heat) offers no altitude edge to either side and is neutral on rest, both teams having played six days prior.

Belgium carry a clear squad quality edge — De Bruyne, Doku, Trossard and a returning Lukaku against a largely domestic Iran side missing Azmoun — and the market's ~65% win probability is broadly fair. A modest discount is warranted because Belgium were unconvincing in a 1-1 draw with Egypt (winless in their last three World Cup matches), and Iran showed fighting spirit twice equalising against New Zealand. Iran's severe logistical handicap — barred from arriving in Los Angeles until 24 hours before kickoff, forced to depart immediately after MD1, and with support staff denied visas — meaningfully suppresses their preparation quality and is a structural drag on their chances.

Uruguay carry a clear class advantage — Valverde, Ugarte, Bentancur, Núñez and Giménez/R.Araújo form a squad well above Cape Verde's level — but two notable injury absences (Ronald Araújo and De Arrascaeta per bet365 team news) blunt their best XI somewhat, and their recent form is uninspiring (one win in five, including a 1-1 against Saudi Arabia).

Egypt hold a clear squad-quality edge anchored by Salah (67 international goals, confirmed to start) and Marmoush, while New Zealand's attack leans heavily on Chris Wood and set-pieces; Egypt are unbeaten in all three prior H2H meetings and bring a rock-solid defensive record (only two goals conceded in their last six matches vs. New Zealand going 12 games without a clean sheet). Both sides are fully fit — New Zealand's only squad concern is the pre-tournament loss of Garbett (hamstring) — and the stakes sharpen the fixture as a genuine six-pointer, which slightly suppresses both teams' attacking intent and keeps draw probability meaningful.

Argentina are overwhelming favourites on every structural dimension: Messi (hat-trick MD1, levelling Klose's all-time World Cup goals record), a settled 4-3-3/4-4-2 with top-club depth across all lines, and a five-game winning streak in which they have scored 12 goals and conceded one. Austria carry a meaningful injury loss in Baumgartner (their most creative midfielder), though Alaba, Laimer, Sabitzer and Seiwald give Rangnick a solid compact 4-2-3-1 that will press high and frustrate — Austria beat Jordan 3-1 but Jordan are a far weaker test.

France are a class apart: ranked 3rd globally vs Iraq's 57th, deploying Mbappé (14 WC career goals, brace vs Senegal), Olise, Dembélé and Barcola in attack against a defence that leaked four to Norway and ranks among the worst at this tournament by xG-against. The only meaningful France concern is Saliba managing a knock, but he is confirmed to start. Iraq lost left-back Ahmed Yahya to a hamstring pre-tournament, their goalkeeper situation is uncertain with Jalal Hassan absent, and they arrive having conceded 4-1 in MD1 with no wins in their last three matches.

Norway enter with the clearer momentum — a 4-1 opening win and Haaland/Ødegaard both fit and firing — while Senegal must bounce back from a 3-1 loss to France. Norway's high-press 4-3-3 is well-suited to dominating the midfield, but Senegal's pace-heavy trio of Sarr, Jackson and Mané poses a genuine counter-attacking threat that could exploit Norway's known defensive frailties. Both sides are fully rested with 7 days since MD1, and the sea-level MetLife venue offers no atmospheric edge to either. A narrow Norway win is the most likely outcome, but Senegal's direct attacking quality keeps a draw or away win firmly on the table, consistent with the market's compressed spread.

Algeria hold a clear edge on squad quality — Mahrez, Amoura, Bensebaini, Ait-Nouri and Maza represent far superior European-club depth to Jordan's largely domestic roster — and their pre-tournament form (7-0 vs Guatemala, 4-0 vs Bolivia, 1-0 vs Netherlands) is significantly stronger than Jordan's run of losses against Switzerland and Colombia. Jordan's MD1 performance against Austria was spirited — matching them in shots (11 vs 11) and equalising before late experience told — but their defence conceded three and has not kept a clean sheet in any of their last five matches, while Algeria's attack has the set-piece and wide-channel tools to exploit exactly those frailties.

Portugal's squad quality overwhelms Uzbekistan's first-ever World Cup squad, and the return of Ruben Dias shores up a defense that leaked a sloppy equalizer in MD1. However, Portugal managed only one shot on target against DR Congo's deep block — a tactical profile Uzbekistan will replicate — introducing genuine draw risk that the market slightly underprices. Martinez's side carries a 'must-win' mentality and the pressure to respond, while Uzbekistan, after failing to touch the ball in Colombia's box in the first half of MD1, look short of the attacking quality needed to nick anything from this game.

England are heavily favoured on all seven factors: superior squad depth with Kane, Bellingham and a near-full complement fit (Rice and Rashford cleared training, only Saka is benched), dominant recent form with a 4-2 win over Croatia setting a World Cup record for shots inside the box, and a sea-level US venue that neutralises any altitude edge. Ghana arrive missing two automatic starters in Kudus (quad injury) and Salisu (ACL), Partey returns from his Canada visa ban but is carrying a groin issue and has no match sharpness, and their weakened defensive spine faces an England attack in confident, high-volume form.

Croatia hold a decisive edge in squad quality, tournament pedigree, and midfield creativity through Modrić and Kovačić, while Panama failed to score against Ghana and lack the individual firepower to trouble a motivated Croatian defence. Both sides are under must-win pressure, which slightly inflates draw probability but ultimately favours Croatia's superior technical quality in a direct elimination-style showdown. The market's ~62% Croatia win probability is well-supported; a small discount is applied given Panama's defensive discipline and the organisational strength Christiansen has instilled, leaving a realistic chance of a low-scoring draw.

Colombia are the clear class act here — a fully fit squad, clinical 3-1 MD1 win with three different scorers, Luis Díaz at peak form, and the altitude/heat of Guadalajara favoring the CONMEBOL side over a DR Congo squad based primarily in Europe. DR Congo's 1-1 draw with Portugal shows real defensive organisation and counter-attacking threat via Bakambu and Wissa, so a shutout is not guaranteed, but Congo need a win themselves which may open them up.

Both teams are level on points with Canada ahead only on goal difference, so a draw serves Canada's interests (they advance in first) while Switzerland must win to top the group — creating asymmetric pressure that slightly favours the Swiss in attacking intent. Canada lose Ismael Kone (fractured leg) and Alfie Jones (ankle), while Alphonso Davies is available only off the bench after his hamstring lay-off, marginally weakening Canada's depth. Switzerland arrive fully fit, five-match unbeaten, and have never conceded in the first half in that run, but BC Place's indoor, climate-controlled conditions eliminate any travel/venue tax and the roaring home crowd is a genuine Canadian edge.

Bosnia carry a meaningful squad quality edge over a Qatar side that is severely disrupted — two key players (Madibo, Ahmed) suspended after the 6-0 Canada meltdown, a worrying defensive record, and zero wins in their last five. Bosnia also have one suspension (Muharemovic) but their replacement options are adequate, and Dzeko/Demirovic provide genuine attacking threat. Critically, both teams are level on one point and a draw almost certainly eliminates them both, so Qatar will attack — narrowing the draw probability and keeping this contest competitive.

Morocco are a class apart in this fixture: top-club talent across the XI (Hakimi, Saibari, El Khannouss), unbeaten in five with only two goals conceded, and a settled system that already held Brazil. Haiti are eliminated regardless of result, have lost both WC matches (0-1 vs Scotland, 0-3 vs Brazil), rank 83rd in the world, and carry no meaningful attacking threat beyond Isidor and Bellegarde against a well-organized Moroccan backline. Atlanta is sea-level, neutral venue with no altitude or travel edge for either side, and both teams have had standard 4-5 days rest; Morocco may rotate fringe players but should still control the match comfortably.

Mexico enter as heavy altitude-and-form favourites — perfect 6/6 points, unbeaten at home in 26 straight internationals, and with captain Montes back from suspension — but Aguirre is expected to rotate significantly with top spot already secured, narrowing the gap versus a desperate Czechia side. Czechia's key injury concern is full-back Jurasek, while the altitude at the Azteca (~2,240m) is a genuine physical tax on European legs and tilts the venue factor firmly toward Mexico.

South Korea hold every structural advantage: superior squad headlined by Son, Kim Min-jae and Lee Kang-in; three points already banked meaning a draw qualifies them; and no injuries or suspensions. South Africa, by contrast, are shorn of midfield linchpin Mokoena (suspended) alongside Zwane, have scored just once in two World Cup matches, and are winless in six. The stakes asymmetry is decisive: South Africa must open up to win, handing Korea's pace in transition ideal conditions to exploit. A narrow Korean victory — likely 1-0 or 1-1 — is the most probable outcome, with the draw kept alive only by South Africa's desperation creating brief moments of pressure.

Brazil enter as heavy favourites with four points already secured and Vinicius Junior on the scoresheet in both games, though they lose Raphinha to a hamstring injury and Raphinha's replacement is the uncapped teenager Rayan; Neymar is fit enough only for a cameo at best, while Rodrygo and Estêvão are absent for the tournament. Scotland sit on three points and need at minimum a draw to keep knockout hopes alive, giving them unusual attacking intent, but they have never beaten Brazil (D2 L8 all-time) and their best creative outlet is the McTominay-McGinn axis against a still-deep Brazilian midfield of Casemiro, Bruno Guimarães and Paquetá.

Germany are a clearly superior side (FIFA top-10, Bundesliga/elite-club depth) who have already qualified as Group E winners after back-to-back wins over Curaçao (7-1) and Ivory Coast (2-1), and they carry strong momentum. Ecuador are winless (0-1 L, 0-0 D), have not scored a single goal in the tournament, and desperately need a win to have any hope of advancing — that desperation opens them up tactically. Germany lose Schlotterbeck to a tournament-ending ankle injury, but Nagelsmann has Rüdiger, Tah, Anton and Thiaw as cover, and may rotate heavily; however, manager comments suggest changes will be limited to forced ones, meaning Germany will still field a competitive XI.

Ivory Coast hold a massive quality edge — AFCON champions with Premier League and Serie A talent throughout, needing only a draw to advance — generating extreme asymmetry in motivation and execution. Curaçao showed gritty defensive resilience (15 saves from Room, 0-0 vs Ecuador), but their 5-4-1 low-block is unlikely to contain Ivory Coast's attack for 90 minutes when the Elephants are pressing for three points to guarantee second place. The minor defensive disruptions for CIV (Singo out, N'Dicka uncertain) are partly offset by Doue slotting in at right-back, and the Philadelphia sea-level venue is neutral for both sides.

Japan enter in sharper form (4-0 win vs Tunisia, unbeaten in five) while Sweden were routed 5-1 by the Netherlands, exposing defensive frailties. Japan's pre-tournament losses of Mitoma, Endo, and now Kubo in-tournament dent their attacking ceiling, but their system under Moriyasu remains cohesive. Sweden's Isak/Gyokeres pairing is elite and makes a shutout unlikely, but their defensive backline looked vulnerable against pace — exactly what Japan's wing-backs and Kamada provide. The market's slight Japan lean is justified; we align closely with it but shade the draw slightly lower given Sweden's pressing need to attack after the Netherlands hammering.

Tunisia are mathematically eliminated, playing under interim manager Hervé Renard after two heavy defeats (1-5, 0-4) with zero shots on target against Japan, signalling a side in total disarray. Netherlands are full of confidence after a 5-1 demolition of Sweden and boast a far superior squad headlined by Van Dijk, De Jong, Gakpo and Dumfries. No suspensions or major injuries affect either side ahead of this fixture, and the Dutch have never lost a World Cup game against an African team. The market's ~85% Dutch win probability is well-supported; we align closely and lean toward a comfortable Netherlands victory.

The USA enter this dead-rubber finale having already clinched top spot in Group D with back-to-back wins (4-1 vs Paraguay, 2-0 vs Australia), playing in their de facto home stadium at SoFi in Los Angeles with massive crowd support. Türkiye are already eliminated after losing both group games without scoring a single goal, exposing a complete failure of their vaunted attack on American soil.

The single biggest factor shifting this away from the market is Miguel Almiron's confirmed suspension after his red card vs Turkey — he is Paraguay's top scorer and primary creative engine, and his absence materially weakens their ability to break down Australia's organised 3/5-back block. Australia carry a minor injury concern over Mathew Leckie (muscle issue vs USA) but have no suspensions and superior FIFA ranking (27th vs 41st), better goal difference, and stronger head-to-head record.

Senegal are significantly stronger on paper and carry enormous desperation — they need a big win to have any realistic chance of advancing as a best third-place team. However, injury concerns to Mendy, Jackson, Sarr and Pape Gueye, combined with an already rattled defensive record (6 goals conceded in 2 games), temper confidence. Iraq are eliminated and may rotate, reducing their threat and defensive resolve, which tips the contest firmly toward Senegal. The market's 74.6% home-win probability is broadly aligned, but we shade slightly lower to account for Senegal's injury-disrupted build-up and psychological fragility.

France's squad depth, defensive solidity (two clean sheets in the group), and Mbappé's form make them clear favourites, broadly aligned with the market's 0.598 away probability. Norway are genuinely dangerous — Haaland's four goals show their direct attacking system works — and both sides are already through, which historically inflates draw likelihood slightly; however, with first place and the Golden Boot on the line for both star forwards, neither camp has strong incentive to coast. The Deschamps absence (Guy Stephan deputising) introduces a minor psychological unknown for France but is unlikely to affect tactical shape or squad selection.

Spain's squad quality and form are clearly superior — a dominant 4-0 win over Saudi Arabia vs. Uruguay's back-to-back draws — and the market correctly makes them heavy favorites. However, Estadio Akron sits at 1,566m altitude, a genuine physiological burden for a sea-level European side, while South American Uruguay adapts more naturally; this compresses the gap meaningfully. Bielsa's Uruguay will be ultra-motivated (elimination pressure) and is tactically disciplined, capable of absorbing pressure with Valverde, Ugarte, and Bentancur in midfield.

Cape Verde enter as the form side on merit: unbeaten in both World Cup matches (0-0 vs Spain, 2-2 vs Uruguay) and have lost only once in their last 17 games across 90 minutes, while Saudi Arabia have just one win in their last eight and were hammered 4-0 by Spain. A key suspension bites Cape Verde — Sidny Lopes Cabral is banned, thinning their defensive cover — but Saudi Arabia carry their own wound: Salem Al-Dawsari and Mohamed Kanno both picked up yellows vs Spain and must stay disciplined, and a manager change mid-tournament (Georgios Donis replaced Renard in April) adds tactical uncertainty.

Egypt lead Group G on 4 points and need only a draw to guarantee historic first-ever knockout qualification, while Iran (2 pts) ultimately need a win to feel safe. Egypt's attacking quality via Salah and Marmoush is a clear differential and their xG/shot metrics vastly outperform Iran's across the tournament; however, two Egyptian defensive starters (Fathi, Abdelmaguid) are ruled out, slightly softening the backline. Iran's ultra-defensive 5-4-1 shape, Beiranvand's heroics in goal, and the oldest starting XI in World Cup history suggest a low-scoring, cagey affair.

Belgium's squad quality (De Bruyne, Courtois, Tielemans, Trossard) vastly outclasses New Zealand, and their tournament survival pressure provides strong motivation to finally convert chances. However, both Doku and Lukaku are confirmed unfit for 90 minutes, and Belgium's two successive draws expose a finishing problem that tempers confidence. New Zealand's entirely fit squad will press hard but has conceded five goals in two matches and lacks the individual quality to hurt a healthy Belgium backline. The draw probability is nudged slightly above the market to reflect Belgium's persistent bluntness in front of goal, but a controlled Belgium away win remains the clear most-likely outcome.

Croatia need a win to advance and carry clear squad-quality edge with Modric, Kovacic and Gvardiol at the core, while Ghana are boosted by a pragmatic draw vs England but are missing Kudus (hamstring) and their first-choice goalkeeper Ati-Zigi — a meaningful double absence for an already limited attacking unit. Ghana's stakes favour containment (a draw sends them through), which inflates draw probability and suppresses Croatia's win probability slightly, but Croatia's greater creative depth and urgent need to win should keep pressure on a patched Ghana backline. Philadelphia is a sea-level, moderate-climate venue with no altitude factor; both sides had four days' rest.

England are heavy favourites and carry genuine motivation: a win likely seals Group L top spot, whereas Panama are already eliminated with no points from five World Cup appearances all-time. The squad-quality gap is vast — Harry Kane, Bellingham, Declan Rice (managed but available), and Saka/Rashford returning to fitness all feature, while Reece James is the only meaningful absentee. Panama have been the lowest-shot-generating side at this tournament and conceded in both group games despite holding compact defensive shapes; England's 4-2 demolition of the same Croatia side that beat Panama 1-0 underlines the class difference.

Portugal's superior squad depth, elite midfield, and explosive recent form (5-0, 11 goals in last 5) make them mild favourites, but Colombia enter with a perfect 6-point record and a massive partisan Miami crowd that Martinez himself acknowledges is 'away from home' for Portugal. Colombia only need a draw for group victory, allowing Lorenzo to set up a disciplined low-block counter-attacking structure that suits Luis Díaz perfectly, inflating the draw probability above the market's 25.7%. Both squads are injury-free, stakes asymmetry slightly blunts Portugal's attacking urgency and compresses the away-win probability modestly versus the book's 45.9%.

DR Congo are the stronger side on paper — better-quality European-based players, a more organised defensive structure (5-3-2 with Mpasi, Mbemba, Wan-Bissaka), and tangibly better in-tournament evidence: a creditable 1-1 draw with Portugal and a narrow 0-1 loss to Colombia versus Uzbekistan's 1-3 and 0-5 capitulations. Both squads are fully available with no injury or suspension concerns reported. The stakes dimension marginally tilts toward Congo DR, who have a viable advancement scenario requiring a win, while Uzbekistan — already bottom with zero points and -7 GD — have nothing to lose, making them dangerous in a 'nothing-to-lose' sense.

This match is uniquely distorted by incentive misalignment: Austria (superior goal difference, currently 2nd) may rationally prefer to lose or draw, while Algeria (3rd, GD -2) is mathematically safest finishing third to avoid Spain — making a draw the ideal outcome for both sides. Both teams know the full knockout bracket before kickoff, creating near-unprecedented strategic pressure to not win. Squad quality slightly favours Austria (Sabitzer, Alaba, Laimer core; Rangnick's organized system) and they held Argentina to only 2-0, while Algeria's 3-0 mauling by Argentina exposed defensive frailties.

Argentina are the world's top-ranked side with a perfect tournament record, five clean sheets, and a +5 goal difference through two matches, while Jordan are eliminated with zero points, having conceded five goals and shown chronic aerial and defensive vulnerabilities. Scaloni will rotate fringe players — with Romero doubtful and Messi likely managed — but even a heavily rotated Argentina XI carries far superior quality than anything Jordan can muster.

Canada have the stronger, deeper squad with in-form striker Jonathan David (hat-trick vs Qatar) and get a boost with Alphonso Davies likely back on the bench, though they lose midfielder Ismael Kone for the tournament and have late fitness doubts over Eustaquio. South Africa are organized and confident after a gritty win over South Korea and welcome midfielder Mokoena back from suspension, but they remain short on cutting edge, especially with Themba Zwane suspended. The neutral, sea-level SoFi venue removes any altitude edge and slightly favors Canada's better individual quality, but South Africa's solid defense and the typical tightness of knockout games keep a draw very live.

Brazil are clearly the stronger side and are in sharp form with two recent 3-0 wins, and even though they are without Raphinha, Rodrygo and Estevao, their attacking depth around Vinicius Jr and Cunha is far superior. Japan are well-organized and dangerous on the press, but losing captain Endo and star winger Mitoma blunts their creativity, and they have never won a World Cup knockout match. The neutral, hot Houston venue and Japan's quality keep a draw or upset live, so a single-goal Brazil win feels most likely rather than a blowout.

Germany have far more squad quality and attacking firepower, with Musiala, Wirtz and Sané supported by a deep bench that includes super-sub Deniz Undav, who has already changed games at this tournament. They lose Schlotterbeck to a tournament-ending injury but Rüdiger steps in capably, while Paraguay are without sent-off attacker Miguel Almirón and have scored just twice in three group games, relying on a low-block defensive approach. Boston is a neutral, sea-level venue with no altitude factor, and both sides have similar rest, so the gap comes down to talent and Germany's ability to break down a deep defense.

The Netherlands carry the better attack and deeper squad, with a fully fit XI led by an in-form Brobbey front three, which makes them rightful slight favorites. But Morocco are extremely well-organized, have conceded little, and own the disciplined block-and-counter profile that has frustrated bigger sides, plus Hakimi as a constant threat. The Dutch defense has looked leaky and rarely keeps clean sheets, so Morocco's counters stay live and a low-scoring grind into extra time is very plausible. Given how even this tie is, the draw after 90 minutes is a real outcome and confidence is low.

Norway deliberately rested Haaland, Ødegaard and most of their first XI in the 4-1 loss to France, so they arrive fresh and at full strength rather than carrying that defeat's form, and yellow cards reset after the group stage so no suspensions apply to either side. Ivory Coast are a talented, quick and physical AFCON-winning team that can hurt Norway's shaky defense, but Norway have the higher squad ceiling and the game's most decisive player in Haaland. The roofed, climate-controlled venue in Arlington is neutral with no altitude or heat edge for either team.

France are clearly the stronger side, ranked third in the world with elite attacking depth (Mbappé, Dembélé, Olise, Doué) and three straight high-scoring group wins, while Sweden scraped through third after being thrashed 5-1 by the Netherlands. Sweden's Gyökeres-Isak strike pairing carries real counter-attacking danger and France have conceded in four of their last five, so an upset or draw isn't far-fetched, but France's quality and depth should win out. I have France slightly below the market to honor knockout-game variance and their leaky recent defending.

Mexico are the stronger side here: they topped Group A with three wins and no goals conceded, are playing at home in Mexico City altitude where they're well-adjusted, have no injury concerns and will get Raul Jimenez back after resting him. Ecuador are dangerous and well-organized, having just upset Germany behind a healthy Moises Caicedo and goal threats in Plata and Valencia, so they can frustrate and punish on the break. Both teams had equal rest, and World Cup knockout games against compact, counter-attacking sides often stay tight, so a low-scoring narrow Mexico win or a draw are both very live outcomes.

England have far superior squad quality and depth, with Kane, Bellingham, Saka and Rashford all fit and fresh after Tuchel rotated through the group stage, and Rice returning after being rested against Panama. DR Congo are a disciplined, organised side built on a back-five low block that frustrated Portugal, and Yoane Wissa (three group-stage goals) is a genuine counter-attacking threat, so a Ghana-style frustrating afternoon is a real risk given England's stop-start form. Indoor Atlanta neutralises any climate edge and both sides rested four days, so the gulf in quality should tell, though Congo's resilience and the draw-prone nature of these games keep this short of a blowout.
